1: David reflects on the contrast: he dwells in a house of cedar (bêt ʾărāzîm) while the ark dwells under a tent/curtains (yôšeḇet taḥat yᵉrîʿôt — lit. “sitting/dwelling under curtain-cloths”). The Solomonic temple later uses cedar extensively (1 Kgs 5-6); the cedar palace (bêt hāʾerez, 2 Sam 5:11) was the pinnacle of royal architecture. David's sense of incongruity is the motivation for what will be the central covenant of the book.
2: Nathan's initial response — ʿăśēh ʾēt kol-ʾăšer billᵉḇāḇᵉḵā kî hāʾĕlōhîm ʿimmāḵ — do everything in your heart, for God is with you. Nathan gives approval based on his own assessment (ʾāmar without “word of the LORD”). This immediately precedes a divine reversal: the same night YHWH corrects Nathan's well-intentioned but premature approval, establishing that the prophet's personal judgment must yield to divine word.
3: wayhî ballayᵉlāh hahûʾ wayhî dᵉḇar-ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-Nātān — that same night the word of God came to Nathan. The immediacy (“that same night”) prevents any possibility that Nathan's approval has standing. The formula dᵉḇar-YHWH (word of the LORD/God) marks authentic prophetic reception — the word that comes to the prophet overrides all prior judgments.
4: lōʾ-ʾattāh tibnel-lî habbayit lāšāḇet — you shall not build a house for me to dwell in. The divine negation is direct and final. The play on bayit (house) begins here: David proposes to build YHWH a bayit (temple); YHWH responds by promising to build David a bayit (dynasty). The word occurs seven times in vv4-14. The negation does not demean David's intention but redirects it toward the next generation.
5: kî lōʾ yāšaḇtî bᵉḇayit miyyôm haʿălōtî ʾet-Yiśrāʾēl ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — I have never dwelt in a house from the day I brought Israel up until this day. YHWH's self-description as one who has moved (wāʾehyeh mēʾōhel ʾel-ʾōhel ûmimmišḵān) from tent to tent and from tabernacle to tabernacle. The terms ʾōhel (tent) and mišḵān (tabernacle, dwelling-place) both appear, acknowledging the Mosaic worship structure while implying that YHWH's presence is not spatially constrained.
6: YHWH asks rhetorically: did I ever say a word to any of the judges (šōpᵉṭîm) — the Deuteronomistic history's term for Israel's pre-monarchic leaders — asking why they had not built him a cedar house? The implied answer is no: the temple was never YHWH's demand but Solomon's commission. This retroactively addresses any sense that the absence of a temple during the judges period was a failure.
7: kōh tōmar lᵉʿaḇdî lᵉḏāwid — thus says the LORD of hosts: the messenger formula introduces the oracle. ṣᵉḇāʾôt (hosts/armies) — the divine title YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt (LORD of Hosts) emphasizes his command over heavenly and earthly armies, particularly appropriate in the context of David's military conquests (ch18). min-hannāweh (from the pasture) — YHWH took David from following the flock (mēʾaḥar haṣṣōʾn) to being prince (nāgîd) — the shepherd-to-king pattern.
8: wāʾehyeh ʿimmᵉḵā bᵉḵōl ʾăšer hālaḵtā — I have been with you wherever you went. This divine accompaniment formula (ʾehyeh ʿimmᵉḵā — I will be/have been with you) echoes the patriarchal covenant language (Gen 26:3; 28:15) and the commission of Moses (Exod 3:12). wᵉʾaḵrittāh ʾet-kol-ʾôyᵉḇeḵā mippānêḵā — I have cut off all your enemies from before you: the hiphil of kārat (to cut off, cut down) — the same verb for making a covenant; YHWH's covenant with David is expressed partly by eliminating his enemies.
9: wᵉśamtî maqôm lᵉʿammî Yiśrāʾēl wᵉnᵉṭaʿtîhû — I will establish a place for my people Israel and plant them. The verb nāṭaʿ (to plant) applied to a people recalls the Exodus-settlement language: Israel is planted in the land as a vine (Ps 80:8-9; Isa 5:1-7). wᵉlōʾ yirgaz ʿôd — they will not be disturbed again: the promise of security is expressed negatively — the absence of the rᵉgaz (trembling, disturbance) that has characterized their existence.
10: wᵉhiknaʿtî ʾet-kol-ʾôyᵉḇeḵā — I will subdue all your enemies. The shift from historical retrospection (vv7-9b) to future promise is marked by waw-consecutive imperfect: wᵉhiknaʿtî (I will humble/subdue). wᵉhiggadtî lāḵ kî-bayit yibne-lᵉḵā YHWH — moreover, I declare to you that the LORD will build you a house: the pivot verse. higgîd (hiphil of nāgad, to declare, announce) is used for authoritative proclamation. The house YHWH promises to build David is a bayit (dynasty), not a building.
11: wᵉhāyāh kî-yimlᵉʾû yāmeḵā lāleḵet ʿim-ʾăḇōteḵā — when the time comes for you to go to be with your ancestors. The idiom lāleḵet ʿim-ʾăḇōteḵā (to go with your fathers) is a euphemism for death without the typical verb mût (to die). wăhăqîmōtî ʾet-zarʿăḵā ʾaḥăreḵā — I will raise up your seed/offspring after you. zeraʿ (seed) is the key term: Abraham received the covenant through his zeraʿ (Gen 17:7-10); David receives the same covenant-language applied now to the royal dynasty.
12: hûʾ yibne-lî bayit wᵉḵōnanᵉtî ʾet-kisʾô ʿad-ʿôlām — he will build a house for me, and I will establish his throne forever. kōnanᵉtî — the piel of kûn, to establish, make firm, set in place. The verb is significant: YHWH himself will be the one to establish the throne — human political skill and military power cannot secure what only divine action can guarantee. ʿad-ʿôlām (forever) appears twice in this oracle (vv12, 14) — the key term of the Davidic covenant's unconditional permanence.
13: ʾănî ʾehyeh-lô lᵉʾāḇ wᵉhûʾ yihyeh-lî lᵉḇēn — I will be his father, and he will be my son. This adoption formula is the most theologically important sentence in ch17. In its immediate context it refers to Solomon; in canonical context it becomes the basis for the NT's identification of Jesus as the eternal Son of God (Ps 2:7; 2 Sam 7:14; Heb 1:5). wᵉḥasdî lōʾ-ʾāsîr mimmennû — I will not remove my steadfast love (ḥesed) from him, as I removed it from Saul. The contrast with Saul is explicit: the Davidic dynasty receives an unconditional ḥesed that the Saulide dynasty never had.
14: wᵉhaʿămadtîhû bᵉḇêtî ûḇᵉmalḵûtî ʿad-hāʿôlām — I will establish him in my house and in my kingdom forever. The Chronicler substitutes malḵûtî (my kingdom) for 2 Sam 7:16's malḵûtᵉḵā (your kingdom). This is a deliberate theological revision: the Chronicler emphasizes that the kingdom belongs to YHWH, not to David. David and his dynasty are stewards of a divine kingdom, not proprietors of a human one. The permanent throne (kisʾô yihyeh nāḵôn ʿad-ʿôlām) is the Davidic throne understood as the earthly manifestation of YHWH's own sovereign rule.
15: Nathan delivered the vision (ḥāzôn) — the same noun used for prophetic visions in Isa 1:1, Nah 1:1. The entire oracle is characterized as a ḥāzôn (vision) rather than merely a verbal message, indicating a higher mode of revelation. kᵉḵōl haddᵉḇārîm hāʾēlleh ûkᵉḵōl haḥāzôn hazzeh — in keeping with all these words and the full vision: Nathan's faithfulness in transmission is noted.
16: wayyāḇōʾ hamelekh dāwid wayyēšeḇ lipnê YHWH — King David went in and sat before the LORD. The posture of sitting (yāšaḇ) before the LORD is unusual — prayer is typically standing or prostrate. The sitting may indicate a prolonged audience or it may carry the connotation of dwelling in God's presence (the same verb, yāšaḇ, used of YHWH dwelling in his sanctuary). mî ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ûmî bêtî — who am I, LORD God, and what is my house? — the humility formula standard in covenant responses (cf. Exod 3:11; Judg 6:15; 1 Sam 18:18).
17: The MT of this verse is difficult: wattiqdaš zōʾt lᵉʿêneḵā hāʾĕlōhîm ûtᵉdabbēr ʿal-bêt-ʿaḇdᵉḵā lᵉmērāḥôq ûrᵉʾîtanî kᵉtôr hāʾādām hammaʿălāh. The phrase kᵉtôr hāʾādām hammaʿălāh is obscure: possibly “according to the manner of a man of high rank” or “like one exalted among men.” The LXX differs significantly. The parallel in 2 Sam 7:19 reads wᵉzōʾt tôrat hāʾādām (and this is the charter/instruction for humanity) — an equally difficult phrase. The MKT renders it as “the highest state of humanity” — acknowledging the difficulty while capturing the sense that YHWH has honored David with something transcendent.
18: mah-yôsîp dāwid ʾēleḵā lᵉḵāḇôd ʾet-ʿaḇdᵉḵā wᵉʾattāh ʾet-ʿaḇdᵉḵā yādāʿtā — what more can David say to you about the honor you have shown your servant? You know your servant. The verb kābad (to honor, make weighty) applied to God's action toward David inverts the normal direction: humans honor God; here God honors the human. wᵉʾattāh ʾet-ʿaḇdᵉḵā yādāʿtā — and you know your servant: yādaʿ here is the intimate knowing of covenant relationship, not merely cognitive awareness.
19: YHWH baʿăḇur ʿaḇdᵉḵā ûkᵉlibbᵉḵā ʿāśîtā ʾēt kol-haggᵉdûllāh hazzōʾt — LORD, for your servant's sake and according to your own heart you have done this great thing. kᵉlibbᵉḵā (according to your heart) — YHWH acts from his own inner purpose and affection, not from external constraint. The phrase ʾet kol-haggᵉdûllāh hazzōʾt (all this greatness) is a summary exclamation for the entirety of the Davidic covenant oracle.
20: YHWH ʾên kāmôḵā wᵉʾên ʾĕlōhîm zûlātᵉḵā — LORD, there is none like you, and there is no God besides you. The incomparability formula (ʾên kāmôḵā) is a standard confession of YHWH's uniqueness in the royal and prophetic literature (cf. Exod 15:11; 1 Kgs 8:23; Isa 40:18). The additional clause wᵉʾên ʾĕlōhîm zûlātᵉḵā (no God besides you) edges toward explicit monotheism — not merely that YHWH is superior among gods but that he is uniquely God.
21: ûmî kᵉʿammᵉḵā Yiśrāʾēl gôy ʾeḥād bāʾāreṣ — and who is like your people Israel, one nation on earth? The incomparability extends from YHWH to Israel: as YHWH is incomparable among gods, Israel is incomparable among nations. ʾăšer hālaḵ hāʾĕlōhîm liphôt lô ʿam — whom God set out to redeem (pādāh, to redeem/ransom) as his own people. nôrāʾôt (awesome deeds) — the same term as v25 of the preceding psalm.
22: wattittēn ʾet-ʿammᵉḵā Yiśrāʾēl lᵉʿammᵉḵā ʿad-ʿôlām wᵉʾattāh YHWH hāyîtā lāhem lēʾlōhîm — you established Israel as your own people forever, and you, LORD, became their God. The mutually constituting relationship: Israel is YHWH's people; YHWH is Israel's God. The covenant formula (ʿam lî / YHWH lᵉʾlōhêhem) appears throughout the covenant literature (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Jer 31:33; Rev 21:3) as the summary of the entire covenant relationship.
23: wᵉʿattāh YHWH haddāḇār ʾăšer dibbartā ʿal-ʿaḇdᵉḵā wᵉʿal-bêtô yēʾāmēn ʿad-ʿôlām — now LORD, let the word you spoke about your servant and his house stand firm forever. The verb ʾāman (hiphil: to be made firm, confirmed, reliable — the root of ʾāmēn) applied to the divine word: David asks that YHWH's word prove itself ʾĕmûnāh (faithfully true) in fulfillment.
24: wᵉyēʾāmēn wᵉyigdal šimḵā ʿad-ʿôlām — may your name be made great forever. The connection between the covenant's stability and YHWH's reputation is explicit: the fulfillment of the Davidic promise is simultaneously a validation of YHWH's name. YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾĕlōhîm lᵉYiśrāʾēl — LORD of Hosts, God of Israel, God to Israel: the triple divine title emphasizes YHWH's comprehensive identity as both cosmic sovereign and covenantal partner.
25: kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhay gillîtā ʾet-ʾōzen ʿaḇdᵉḵā libᵉnôt-lô bayit — for you, my God, have revealed to your servant that you will build him a house. gillāh ʾōzen (uncovered the ear, revealed) — the idiom for private disclosure of privileged information (1 Sam 20:2; 2 Sam 7:27). The uncovering of the ear is an intimate act: YHWH has taken David into his confidence about his own plans. ʿal-kēn māṣāʾ ʿaḇdᵉḵā lᵉhitpallēl lᵉpāneḵā — that is why your servant has found it possible to pray before you: the prayer is not a request but a response to prior revelation.
26: wᵉʿattāh YHWH ʾattāh-hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm wattidabbēr ʾet-hattôḇāh hazzōʾt ʾel-ʿaḇdᵉḵā — now LORD, you are God (ʾattāh-hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm — an identification formula: you are the one who is God), and you have spoken this good thing to your servant. The affirmation grounds the following petition: because YHWH is God and has spoken, his word will be fulfilled.
27: wᵉʿattāh hôʾalt lᵉḇārēk ʾet-bêt ʿaḇdᵉḵā lihyôt lᵉʿôlām lᵉpāneḵā — now please be pleased to bless the house of your servant, that it may stand before you forever. hôʾalt — the hiphil of yāʾal (to be willing, be pleased to) — David asks that YHWH's willingness (rāṣôn in concept) express itself in blessing. kî-ʾattāh YHWH bēraḵtā ûmᵉḇōrāḵ yihyeh lᵉʿôlām — for you, LORD, have blessed it, and it will be blessed forever: the prayer closes on certainty — what YHWH has blessed remains blessed.
